1.synthesis: Daniel 12 is the eschatological apex of the entire book, answering the question that ch. 11’s tribulation raises: what happens at the end of the time of distress? The answer is threefold. First: Michael, the great prince who watches over Israel, will arise—the cosmic guardian who has been fighting the Persian and Greek princes throughout chs. 10–11 now rises in decisive action. Second: there will be a time of distress such as has never occurred since nations came into being (meʾaz hayah goy)—the language echoes Joel 2:2 and is applied by Jesus to the events surrounding Jerusalem’s fall in Matthew 24:21. And at that time—“your people will be rescued, everyone who is found written in the book.” Third, and most momentous: “Multitudes who sleep in the dust of the earth will awake: some to everlasting life, others to shame and everlasting contempt.” This is the OT’s clearest, most explicit statement of a general resurrection with two outcomes, anticipating the NT’s doctrine of resurrection and final judgment. The maskilim who instructed many (11:33) shine like the brightness of the heavens; those who led many to righteousness, like the stars forever and ever. The scroll is then sealed until the time of the end.
1.historical_context: Daniel 12:2 is the most theologically significant verse in chs. 10–12 for its contribution to OT anthropology and eschatology. The OT generally describes Sheol as the undifferentiated realm of the dead (cf. Ecclesiastes 9:5; Psalm 88:10), with only hints of resurrection hope (Isaiah 26:19: “your dead shall live; their bodies shall rise”; Ezekiel 37:1–14’s dry bones, though read as national restoration). Daniel 12:2 is the clearest pre-NT statement of individual resurrection with differentiated destinies: chayyey olam (everlasting life) and diʾon olam (everlasting contempt). The Maccabean martyrs clearly understood this verse as their comfort: 2 Maccabees 7 records the seven brothers dying under Antiochus IV with explicit confidence in resurrection. The sealing of the scroll (v. 4) contrasts with Revelation 22:10’s “do not seal the words of the prophecy of this book, for the time is near”—progressive disclosure between Daniel and Revelation.
1.literary_structure: Four verses that move from the cosmic action (Michael rises, v. 1) through the great distress and rescue (v. 1b) to the resurrection announcement (v. 2), the maskilim’s reward (v. 3), and the sealing command (v. 4). The structure mirrors the book’s overall movement from tribulation to vindication: every section of Daniel that describes persecution ends with the saints vindicated. Here, the vindication is ultimate—not merely political restoration but resurrection to everlasting life. The instruction to seal the scroll and the prediction that “many will go here and there to increase knowledge” (v. 4) frame the end of the vision with a note of temporal distance: what is disclosed is for a future that Daniel cannot fully comprehend.
1.theological_application: The resurrection promise of v. 2 transforms the context for the maskilim’s suffering described in ch. 11: if those who “fall by sword and flame, by captivity and plunder” (11:33) wake to everlasting life, then the tribulation is not the last word. The two-destiny resurrection—everlasting life and everlasting contempt—establishes the eschatological accountability that the visions have been gesturing toward throughout: the little horn who prevails against the saints for a time, the willful king who honors himself above all gods, the apostates who forsake the covenant—all will face the judgment implied by the two-destiny resurrection. Communities that endure tribulation for faithfulness are invited by this verse to locate their hope not in this-worldly vindication but in the resurrection that those who “sleep in the dust” will experience.
1.christology: Daniel 12:2 is the OT verse most directly cited in the NT for the doctrine of resurrection. John 5:28–29 echoes it almost verbatim: “an hour is coming when all who are in the tombs will hear his voice and come out, those who have done good to the resurrection of life, and those who have done evil to the resurrection of judgment.” Jesus presents himself as the one who speaks the word that raises the dead (John 5:25)—the voice of the Son of Man (cf. Daniel 7:13) that the dead will hear. The shining of the maskilim like stars forever (v. 3) becomes in Matthew 13:43 the description of the righteous in the Father’s kingdom after the final judgment. The resurrection of Daniel 12:2 is the OT’s anticipation of the resurrection of Christ, which is “the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep” (1 Corinthians 15:20).

5.synthesis: The book of Daniel closes with a scene of two angelic witnesses flanking the river and one asking the figure clothed in linen: “How long will it be before these astonishing things are fulfilled?” The man clothed in linen raises both hands to heaven—the oath gesture—and swears by the one who lives forever: “a time, times, and half a time (lemoed moedim vahetz).” When the shattering of the power of the holy people comes to an end, all these things will be completed. Daniel hears but does not understand and asks for clarification; the messenger responds that the words are sealed until the time of the end. The wicked will continue to act wickedly, but the wise will understand. Specific numbers are given: from the abolition of the daily sacrifice and the setting up of the abomination of desolation, there will be 1,290 days; blessed is the one who waits and arrives at the 1,335 days. The two numbers (1,290 and 1,335) add 30 and 75 days respectively to the 1,260 days of “time, times, and half a time.” Their precise referents remain debated. The book’s final word to Daniel is personal and pastoral: “Go your way till the end. You will rest, and then at the end of the days you will rise to receive your allotted inheritance.” The book that began with Daniel taken from Jerusalem into exile ends with Daniel promised an individual resurrection and an inheritance in the land.
5.historical_context: The two angelic witnesses flanking the river echo the legal requirement of two witnesses for establishing testimony (Deuteronomy 19:15): the oath sworn by the linen-clad figure is witnessed and legally valid. The oath by “the one who lives forever (lehay ha-olam)” is an oath by the eternal God, of the highest possible authority. The “time, times, and half a time” (first stated in 7:25) recurs here as the final measure of the period of the shattering of the holy people. The 1,290 and 1,335 days have generated extensive calculation: if the 1,260 days represent 3½ years from the abomination’s setting up to the rededication of the sanctuary (December 167 to December 164 BCE), the additional 30 and 75 days may refer to subsequent events in the Maccabean period or point to an eschatological extension beyond the historical Antiochene fulfillment. Daniel’s final rest and rising (tinaḥ vetamod legoral laqetz hayamin) is one of the most personal resurrection promises in the OT: the prophet who received all these visions is himself promised participation in the resurrection he disclosed.
5.literary_structure: The closing section (vv. 5–13) forms a literary frame with the opening of the vision (10:1–9): both feature the Tigris/river setting, the linen-clad figure, and Daniel’s prostration. The intervening revelation (10:10–12:4) is sealed by these river scenes. The structure of vv. 5–13: angelic question (v. 6), oath-answer (v. 7), Daniel’s question (v. 8), refusal to clarify (vv. 9–10), numerical specifications (vv. 11–12), final personal word to Daniel (v. 13). The book ends not with a universal declaration but with a personal promise to the individual prophet—a pastoral word to the man who has borne the weight of all these visions. The sealing of the scroll and the promise of rest and rising places Daniel’s personal fate within the very eschatological framework his visions have disclosed.
5.theological_application: The final word to Daniel—“go your way till the end”—is the pastoral word to every community that has heard the eschatological revelations of Daniel: continue in ordinary faithfulness until the end comes. The visions do not exempt their recipients from the ordinary path; Daniel is not translated to heaven or given a special commission for the final age. He is told to go his way, to rest (in death), and to rise (at the resurrection) to receive his inheritance. The beatitude of v. 12 (“blessed is the one who waits for and reaches the end of the 1,335 days”) is the book’s final stance toward the tribulation it has described: patient endurance, waiting in faith, is the path of blessing. The book of Daniel begins with a crisis of faithfulness under pressure (chs. 1–6) and ends with the promise that faithful waiting reaches a blessed end.
5.christology: Daniel’s final promise—“you will rise to receive your allotted inheritance (goral)”—is the OT’s clearest promise of personal resurrection to a named individual. The word goral (lot/allotment) is the covenant-land inheritance language of Joshua’s tribal allotments; here it is applied to Daniel’s eschatological portion in the renewed creation. Christ’s resurrection is the firstfruits that guarantees Daniel’s goral: “as in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive. But each in his own order: Christ the firstfruits, then at his coming those who belong to Christ” (1 Corinthians 15:22–23). The sealed book of Daniel is unsealed in Revelation, where the Lamb who is worthy to open the seals is the same risen Lord whose resurrection guarantees every “you will rise” spoken to the faithful dead.
